exercises.title: FORS Security Calculation
exercises.description: Calculate FORS forgery probabilities under different usage scenarios.
tasks: Calculate forgery probability after 1, 10, 100 signatures for k=33, a=6
Determine maximum safe signatures before probability exceeds 2^{-64}
Calculate expected FORS reuse in SLH-DSA with h=64 after 2^32 total signatures
Compare FORS security with WOTS+ (one-time signature)
Design parameters (k, a) for a FORS with 2^{-128} forgery after 1000 uses
exercises.expectedOutput: Security calculations with clear probability analysis
hints: P(forge after q) ≈ (q/2^a)^k assuming uniform distribution
FORS reuse probability: birthday paradox with 2^h buckets

exercises.title: Multi-Target Attack Analysis
exercises.description: Analyze multi-target attack resistance in hash-based signatures.
tasks: Explain why naive Lamport signatures are vulnerable to multi-target attacks
Show how PK.seed provides domain separation in SLH-DSA
Calculate attack complexity with 2^20 users for schemes with/without domain separation
Design a multi-target attack on a hypothetical scheme without PK.seed
Prove that adding user-specific salt to all hashes prevents multi-target speedup
hints: Without salt: find preimage of ANY pk[i] among N users
With N targets and 2^n security: attack cost ~2^n/N without salt
PK.seed makes each user's hash function independent

exercises.title: Side-Channel Attack Surface Comparison
exercises.description: Compare side-channel attack surfaces of SLH-DSA vs ML-DSA.
tasks: List all secret-dependent operations in SLH-DSA signing
List all secret-dependent operations in ML-DSA signing
Identify which operations are most vulnerable to timing attacks
Design timing attack on non-constant-time hash implementation
Propose mitigations for each identified vulnerability
hints: SLH-DSA secrets: sk_seed, sk_prf, derived WOTS/FORS keys
ML-DSA secrets: s₁, s₂, rejection sampling iterations
Timing leaks: cache-based (array indexing), branch-based (if/else on secrets)

exercises.title: Security Level Comparison Tool
exercises.description: Build a tool to compare security levels across signature schemes.
tasks: Implement security level calculation for classical and quantum attacks
Compare RSA, ECDSA, ML-DSA, and SLH-DSA
Factor in years of cryptanalysis as a confidence metric
Generate security recommendations based on validity period
Output formatted comparison report
exercises.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import math

@dataclass
class SchemeSecurityProfile:
    name: str
    classical_bits: int
    quantum_bits: Optional[int]  # None if broken by quantum
    assumption_type: str
    years_studied: int
    side_channel_difficulty: str  # 'low', 'medium', 'high'

SCHEMES = {
    'RSA-2048': SchemeSecurityProfile(
        'RSA-2048', 112, None, 'Integer Factorization', 45, 'high'
    ),
    'ECDSA-P256': SchemeSecurityProfile(
        'ECDSA P-256', 128, None, 'Discrete Log (EC)', 35, 'high'
    ),
    'ML-DSA-65': SchemeSecurityProfile(
        'ML-DSA-65', 192, 163, 'Module-LWE/SIS', 15, 'high'
    ),
    'SLH-DSA-192s': SchemeSecurityProfile(
        'SLH-DSA-192s', 192, 96, 'Hash Functions', 30, 'medium'
    ),
}

def calculate_security_margin(scheme: SchemeSecurityProfile, 
                               validity_years: int,
                               assume_quantum: bool = False) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate security margin for a scheme.
    
    Args:
        scheme: Security profile
        validity_years: How long signatures must remain secure
        assume_quantum: Whether to assume quantum computers exist
    
    Returns:
        Dictionary with security analysis
    """
    # Your implementation
    pass

def recommend_scheme(min_security_bits: int,
                    validity_years: int,
                    quantum_resistant: bool = True,
                    confidence_priority: bool = False) -> str:
    """
    Recommend best scheme based on requirements.
    
    Args:
        min_security_bits: Minimum required security level
        validity_years: Required validity period
        quantum_resistant: Must be quantum resistant
        confidence_priority: Prefer most conservative assumptions
    
    Returns:
        Name of recommended scheme
    """
    # Your implementation
    pass

# Test recommendations
print("Scheme Recommendations:")
print("=" * 50)

scenarios = [
    (128, 10, True, False),   # Standard web PKI
    (192, 30, True, False),   # Government long-term
    (128, 50, True, True),    # Archival, max confidence
    (128, 5, False, False),   # Legacy compatibility OK
]

for sec, years, qr, conf in scenarios:
    result = recommend_scheme(sec, years, qr, conf)
    print(f"\n{sec}-bit, {years} years, QR={qr}, Confidence={conf}")
    print(f"  Recommended: {result}")
exercises.expectedOutput: Working security comparison tool with recommendations
hints: Factor in Moore's law for classical attack cost over time
Quantum security should be conservative (assume progress)
Confidence metric: years_studied / 30 (normalize to SHA-2 history)
